title.emphasis: 3. Creation for the Public Domain: Government Works

section.para: A distinct scenario arises when IP is created for a government entity. The legal and economic outcome in these cases can represent the most complete form of contribution to the public good.

listitem.para: Works by Federal Employees: Under U.S. copyright law, a "work of the United States Government" is defined as a work prepared by a federal officer or employee as part of their official duties.25 Such works are not entitled to copyright protection in the U.S. and are automatically placed in the public domain.26 This is a clear-cut case where IP is created for society, is entirely free of IP restrictions, and involves no additional tax or regulatory burden beyond the creator's normal employment.

Works from Federally Funded Research: The Bayh-Dole Act governs IP created through federally funded research. It allows universities and small businesses to retain the title to their inventions but grants the federal government a "nonexclusive, nontransferable, irrevocable, paid-up license" to use the invention.28 This arrangement ensures that the public, having funded the research, retains a material interest and right to use the resulting IP. While the creator can commercialize the IP, the public's contribution is secured through this permanent, royalty-free license, reflecting a form of societal mutual aid. This framework, however, does involve significant administrative and reporting requirements.28
